questions.questionText: The structure lying most posteriorly in the hilum of the left lung is:
questions.explanation: At the left lung hilum from anterior to posterior: Pulmonary veins (anterior-most), Pulmonary artery, Bronchus (posterior-most). Memory aid: Left hilum from top to bottom = A-P-V (Artery, bronchus [Pulmonary], Vein). From front to back = V-A-B (Vein, Artery, Bronchus). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Lungs.

questions.questionText: The right bronchus is more prone to aspiration of foreign bodies because it is:
questions.explanation: Right main bronchus: shorter (2.5 cm), wider, more vertical (25° from midline) — making foreign bodies more likely to enter it. Left bronchus: longer (5 cm), narrower, more horizontal (45° from midline) — crosses behind aortic arch. EXAM TRAP: In supine position, aspiration goes to right lower lobe (posterior basal segment). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Bronchial Tree.

questions.questionText: The costodiaphragmatic recess extends from which rib in the midclavicular line?
questions.explanation: The costodiaphragmatic recess (pleural recess where costal and diaphragmatic parietal pleura meet without lung) extends from the 8th to 10th rib in midclavicular line, 10th to 12th in midaxillary line. The lung extends 2 ribs above the pleural reflection. This recess fills during deep inspiration. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Pleura.

questions.questionText: Intercostal nerve, artery, and vein in an intercostal space lie in the order (from above downwards):
questions.explanation: The neurovascular bundle lies in the costal groove of the upper rib, protected between internal and innermost intercostal muscles. Order from above down: VAN (Vein, Artery, Nerve). Memory aid: 'VAN' from top down. During chest tube insertion/intercostal procedures, insert at upper border of lower rib to avoid the bundle. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Intercostal Space.

questions.questionText: The structure that crosses the arch of the aorta from left to right is:
questions.explanation: The left brachiocephalic vein is the longest brachiocephalic vein and crosses from left to right, anterior to the arch of aorta branches, to join the right brachiocephalic vein forming the SVC. Left vagus nerve crosses the arch laterally, giving off the left recurrent laryngeal nerve. Left phrenic nerve is in front of the hilum. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Superior Mediastinum.

questions.questionText: The left recurrent laryngeal nerve hooks around the:
questions.explanation: Left recurrent laryngeal nerve (branch of left vagus) hooks around the arch of aorta lateral to ligamentum arteriosum, then ascends in the tracheoesophageal groove. Right RLN hooks around the right subclavian artery. The left RLN has a longer course and is more vulnerable to damage (e.g., in aortic aneurysm, lung carcinoma). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Thoracic Nerves.

questions.questionText: The most common congenital heart defect is:
questions.explanation: VSD (most commonly in the membranous part of interventricular septum) is the most common congenital heart defect overall. ASD is the most common congenital defect presenting in adults (especially ostium secundum type). TOF is the most common cyanotic CHD. PDA is associated with rubella and prematurity. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Heart Development.

questions.questionText: The thoracic duct drains into the junction of:
questions.explanation: The thoracic duct drains into the left venous angle (junction of left subclavian and left internal jugular veins). It drains lymph from the entire body EXCEPT the right upper limb, right side of head/neck, and right side of thorax (these drain via the right lymphatic duct). It is the largest lymphatic vessel (38-45 cm). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Lymphatic System.

questions.questionText: The SA node is supplied by the right coronary artery in approximately what percentage of cases?
questions.explanation: SA node is supplied by the SA nodal artery, which arises from the RCA in ~60% of cases and from the LCx in ~40%. AV node is supplied by the RCA in ~85% of cases (from posterior descending artery in a right-dominant circulation). EXAM TRAP: SA node RCA ~60%, AV node RCA ~85-90%. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Coronary Arteries.

questions.questionText: The structure lying immediately behind the manubrium sterni is:
questions.explanation: Thymus lies in the anterior mediastinum, immediately behind the manubrium sterni and in front of the pericardium. It is largest relative to body size at birth, reaches maximum absolute size at puberty, then undergoes fatty involution. Thymoma is associated with myasthenia gravis. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Anterior Mediastinum.

questions.questionText: The oblique sinus of the pericardium is bounded by:
questions.explanation: The oblique sinus is a cul-de-sac behind the left atrium, bounded by the pulmonary veins (2 left, 2 right) and the IVC. It is clinically important as pericardial effusion collects here. The transverse sinus lies between the aorta/pulmonary trunk anteriorly and the SVC/pulmonary veins posteriorly — used to pass a clamp during cardiac surgery. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Pericardium.

questions.questionText: For intercostal drainage (chest tube insertion), the tube is inserted at the:
questions.optionA: Lower border of the upper rib
questions.optionB: Upper border of the lower rib
questions.explanation: Chest tube is inserted at the upper border of the lower rib to avoid the intercostal neurovascular bundle (VAN), which lies in the costal groove along the lower border of the upper rib. The safe triangle for chest drain insertion is bounded by: anterior border of latissimus dorsi, lateral border of pectoralis major, and a horizontal line at the 5th intercostal space. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Intercostal Space Applied.

questions.questionText: The azygos vein drains into the:
questions.explanation: Azygos vein arches over the root of the right lung at T4 level and drains into the posterior aspect of the SVC. It connects the IVC to the SVC, providing an alternate route for blood return when either is obstructed (azygos system of emergency veins). Hemiazygos and accessory hemiazygos veins cross the midline to drain into the azygos. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Posterior Mediastinum.

questions.questionText: The number of bronchopulmonary segments in the right lung is:
questions.explanation: Right lung: 3 lobes, 10 bronchopulmonary segments. Left lung: 2 lobes, 8-10 segments (usually counted as 8-9 because lingular division replaces the middle lobe). Each segment has its own segmental bronchus and artery. Segments are functionally independent and can be surgically resected (segmentectomy). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Bronchopulmonary Segments.

questions.questionText: The phrenic nerve is derived from:
questions.explanation: Phrenic nerve arises from C3, C4, C5 (mainly C4). Memory aid: 'C3, 4, 5 keeps the diaphragm alive.' It is the sole motor supply to the diaphragm and also provides sensory innervation to the central diaphragm (pericardium via phrenic, peripheral diaphragm via intercostals). Right phrenic nerve is more vertical. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Cervical Plexus.

questions.questionText: The structure that causes a notch on the barium swallow esophagogram is:
questions.explanation: The esophagus has 4 constrictions/impressions: (1) cricopharyngeus (C6, 15cm from incisors), (2) aortic arch (T4), (3) left main bronchus (T5), (4) diaphragmatic hiatus (T10). Both the aortic arch and left atrium (posterior relationship) cause indentations on barium swallow. Left atrial enlargement causes posterior displacement of the esophagus. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Esophagus Relations.

questions.questionText: The right coronary artery supplies all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: RCA supplies: SA node (60%), AV node (85%), right atrium, right ventricle, posterior 1/3 of IVS, inferior wall of left ventricle (via PDA in right dominant — 85%). The anterior wall of the left ventricle is supplied by the LAD (left anterior descending artery), branch of the left coronary artery. LAD is the 'artery of sudden death.' Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Coronary Circulation.

questions.questionText: The narrowest part of the male urethra is at the:
questions.explanation: The external urethral meatus is the narrowest part of the male urethra (and also the least dilatable). The membranous urethra is the narrowest lumen of the 'INTERNAL' parts but is commonly confused with the meatus. The prostatic urethra is the widest and most dilatable part. EXAM TRAP: Narrowest overall = external meatus, narrowest internal = membranous. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Male Urethra.

questions.questionText: The sternal angle (of Louis) corresponds to which vertebral level?
questions.explanation: The sternal angle (angle of Louis, manubriosternal junction) corresponds to T4/T5 vertebral level. Important landmarks at this level: 2nd costal cartilage articulation, bifurcation of trachea, aortic arch begins/ends, azygos vein drains into SVC, thoracic duct crosses from right to left, upper limit of superior mediastinum. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Surface Anatomy.

questions.questionText: The ligamentum arteriosum is a remnant of:
questions.explanation: Ligamentum arteriosum is the remnant of the ductus arteriosus (connects pulmonary trunk to aortic arch in fetal life). It lies between the left pulmonary artery and the inferior aspect of the aortic arch. The left recurrent laryngeal nerve hooks around it. Patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) causes a continuous 'machinery' murmur. Treatment: Indomethacin (closes PDA). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Fetal Remnants.

questions.questionText: Cardiac tamponade is diagnosed clinically by Beck's triad, which includes all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Beck's triad of cardiac tamponade: (1) Hypotension (decreased cardiac output), (2) Distended neck veins (JVP raised due to impaired venous return), (3) Muffled/distant heart sounds. Pulse pressure is NARROW (not wide) in tamponade. Pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg drop in systolic BP during inspiration) is also characteristic. Treatment: pericardiocentesis. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Pericardium Applied.

questions.questionText: The posterior mediastinum contains all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Posterior mediastinum contains: descending thoracic aorta, esophagus (lower 2/3), azygos system, thoracic duct, sympathetic trunk, vagus nerve, splanchnic nerves. Thymus is in the anterior (superior) mediastinum. EXAM TRAP: Trachea and SVC are in the superior mediastinum, not posterior. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Mediastinal Divisions.

questions.questionText: The structure that passes through the esophageal hiatus of the diaphragm along with the esophagus is:
questions.explanation: Esophageal hiatus (T10): esophagus + anterior vagal trunk (left vagus) + posterior vagal trunk (right vagus) + esophageal branches of left gastric artery. Aortic hiatus (T12): aorta, thoracic duct, azygos vein. Caval opening (T8): IVC, right phrenic nerve branches. Memory aid: 'IVC 8, Esophagus 10, Aorta 12.' Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Diaphragm.

questions.questionText: A patient with Pancoast tumor presents with Horner's syndrome. The structure compressed is:
questions.explanation: Pancoast tumor (superior sulcus tumor, usually NSCLC) at the lung apex compresses: (1) Sympathetic trunk/stellate ganglion → Horner's syndrome (miosis, ptosis, anhidrosis, enophthalmos), (2) Lower trunk of brachial plexus (C8-T1) → hand weakness, (3) Subclavian vessels. Recurrent laryngeal nerve compression causes hoarseness (more common with left lung tumors). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Thoracic Inlet.

questions.questionText: The surface marking of the apex of the heart is located at:
questions.optionD: Left 5th intercostal space, anterior axillary line
questions.explanation: The apex of the heart (formed by the left ventricle) is located in the left 5th intercostal space, just medial to the midclavicular line (approximately 9 cm from the midline). The apex beat is the lowest and most lateral point where the cardiac impulse can be palpated. It is displaced in cardiomegaly, pleural effusion, and pneumothorax. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Surface Anatomy of Heart.

questions.questionText: The trachea bifurcates into the two main bronchi at the level of:
questions.explanation: Tracheal bifurcation occurs at the sternal angle/angle of Louis (T4-T5 vertebral level) at the carina. During deep inspiration, it may descend to T6. The carina is the internal ridge at the bifurcation — widening of the carina on bronchoscopy suggests subcarinal lymphadenopathy (often from lung cancer metastasis). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Trachea.
